buildos-vs-monday-thought-organization.description: Monday.com manages projects brilliantly. But it assumes you already know what to build. Here's what happens before tasks hit the board.
buildos-vs-monday-thought-organization.author: DJ Wayne

faq.a: Yes. monday AI can summarize conversations, generate text for updates, auto-fill column values, and query board data in natural language. However, it operates within your existing board structure — it helps you work with data that is already organized, but cannot create structure from unstructured thoughts or brain dumps.

faq.q: How much does Monday.com cost for one person?
faq.a: Monday.com has a free plan for up to 2 seats. Paid plans use seat bundles that start at 3 seats. At current annual list pricing, Standard is about $12 per seat per month and Pro is about $19 per seat per month, so a solo user effectively starts around $36/month on Standard or about $57/month on Pro.

faq.q: Can I use Monday.com and BuildOS together?
faq.a: Yes, and many knowledge workers benefit from using both. Use BuildOS for the thinking phase — processing scattered thoughts, organizing ideas, and figuring out what to build. Then move well-defined projects into Monday.com for team execution, tracking, and collaboration.

faq.q: What is the difference between project management and thought organization?
faq.a: Project management tools like Monday.com assume you already know what needs to be done and help you coordinate execution. Thought organization tools like BuildOS help you process scattered ideas, concerns, and half-formed plans into clear projects and tasks — the thinking phase that happens before project management begins.

faq.q: Is Monday.com good for individuals or solo workers?
faq.a: Monday.com is primarily designed for team coordination. Its core strengths — shared boards, dependency tracking, workload views, and stakeholder dashboards — serve teams of 3 or more. Solo users can use it, but the 3-seat minimum pricing and team-focused features make it less ideal for individual productivity.

It's a specific category with specific properties — the surface where you author your own mind, where context persists, and where what you gave it yesterday shapes what you get today. Commonplace books and Zettelkastens were analog versions. The digital version is about to matter more than anyone expects.

This post names the habit, names the three feelings (curiosity collapse, feed paranoia, algorithm-shaped thoughts) you have when you've stopped doing it, and ends with a 60-second test that tells you how far gone it is for you.

Here's why it matters for your mind — and what you can do about it.

seo.title: Tech PM Workflow Architecture: Stop Managing Tickets, Start Managing Thinking
seo.description: The #1 reason tech projects stall isn't bad engineers or bad tools — it's scattered thinking. Here's how to fix your workflow architecture.

faq.q: What is the biggest productivity killer for tech project managers?
faq.a: Context fragmentation — project thinking scattered across multiple tools like Slack, Jira, Confluence, and Google Docs. Research shows knowledge workers toggle between apps 1,200 times per day, and it takes roughly 23 minutes to refocus after each interruption.

faq.q: What are appetites in project management and how do they replace estimates?
faq.a: Appetites come from Basecamp's Shape Up methodology. Instead of asking "how long will this take?" you ask "how much time is this problem worth?" Time becomes a design constraint that drives creative scoping decisions, rather than a deadline imposed after estimation theater.

faq.q: How do decision logs improve engineering team productivity?
faq.a: Decision logs capture the choices your team made and why — not task completion percentages. They survive people leaving, prevent teams from re-deriving the same conclusions, and compound over time into an intellectual history that makes every future decision faster.

faq.q: What is impact mapping and how does it connect goals to engineering tasks?
faq.a: Impact mapping (from Gojko Adzic) creates a chain from Goal to Actors to Impacts to Deliverables. Every ticket traces back to an actor and a behavior change, so engineers understand why they're building something — not just what to build.

faq.q: How do you get an engineering team to start writing things down?
faq.a: Start by being the scribe yourself — write the decision records after meetings, and the team's only job is to correct inaccuracies. Find the question that gets asked three times a week, write one document answering it, and share the link next time someone asks. Frame documentation as meeting elimination, not more writing.

30mpc-multithreading-buying-committee.title: Multithreading and the Buying Committee: Lessons from 30MPC and Brent Adamson
30mpc-multithreading-buying-committee.description: A deep read of the 30 Minutes to President's Club multithreading playbook and Brent Adamson's Mobilizer typology — the Golden Path (Champion → Department Leads → Power), why the rep who gets to power first wins the deal, the buyer-benefit framing that earns stakeholder loop-ins, and the 6.8-buyer committee data from Gartner that makes solo-decision-maker outreach a 2010s artifact.
30mpc-multithreading-buying-committee.author: DJ Wayne
30mpc-multithreading-buying-committee.excerpt: A modern B2B purchase is decided by 6 to 11 people, not one. The rep who gets to power first wins the deal — but only if they earn the loop-in by framing it as protecting the buyer from internal collapse, not accelerating the seller's close. 30MPC's Golden Path and Brent Adamson's Mobilizer / Talker / Blocker typology give the practical map. Cold outreach to a single contact in a 7-person committee is gambling.

aaron-shepherd-volume-front-end-offer.author: DJ Wayne
aaron-shepherd-volume-front-end-offer.excerpt: Send a small list of perfect-fit leads and you cannot tell whether good or bad results were luck or signal. Volume is data. After 1.5 million cold emails in 30 days, Aaron Shepherd's framework collapses to three pillars: send enough to get statistical signal (and filter quality on the back end with a pre-call form), wrap whatever you sell in a front-end offer that feels like a test drive, and write scripts so casual they look like they were typed from a phone.

april-dunford-sales-pitch-framework.author: DJ Wayne
april-dunford-sales-pitch-framework.excerpt: A sales pitch is not a product expo. It's a two-part story — The Setup (market context, alternatives, perfect-world scenario) and The Follow-Through (introduction, differentiated value, proof, objections, ask) — designed to align the prospect with your worldview before you ever describe what you sell.

austin-schneider-engagement-first-cold-e.description: A deep read of Instantly's "The New Way of Cold Emailing in 2026" — why AI spam filters killed the seven-touch playbook, the deliverability infrastructure floor (5 emails per domain, ~30/day each), micro-targeted value campaigns (the under-50-recipient reply-rate cliff), and the two-touch rule with non-responder recycling.
austin-schneider-engagement-first-cold-e.author: DJ Wayne
austin-schneider-engagement-first-cold-e.excerpt: Gmail rolled out AI spam filters in 2024. Microsoft followed in 2025. The seven-touch, 10,000-emails-a-month playbook that worked in 2022–2023 now gets you flagged as a bulk sender. The 2026 version is engagement-first: deliverability infrastructure first, micro-targeted value campaigns second, a strict two-touch rule third — and the value in the email has to be an actual deliverable, not "worth a chat."

becc-holland-personalization-to-relevanc.description: A deep read of Becc Holland's relevance framework — the four relevance types (Firmographic, Demographic, Technographic, Trigger-based), the three trigger sub-types (Inbound, Postbound, Bridgebound), and the "if you can send it to two people, it's relevance not personalization" test that separates real research from decorative AI-generated openers.
becc-holland-personalization-to-relevanc.author: DJ Wayne
becc-holland-personalization-to-relevanc.excerpt: Becc Holland's framework cuts through the personalization-vs-template debate that AI made worse. Personalization is 1:1 — research no other prospect can use. Relevance is 1:many — a real reason this message fits a class of buyers right now. Cold outreach that confuses the two ships decorative "I saw you went to State" openers attached to commodity offers. The fix is to grade your hook against four relevance types, anchored in a trigger.

connor-murray-cold-email-assumptive-cade.seoTitle: Less Is More Cold Email: Connor Murray
connor-murray-cold-email-assumptive-cade.description: A deep read of Connor Murray's "10 Years of Expert Cold Email Advice in 36 Minutes" — the three-paragraph body, assumptive language replacement, the coiled-spring prep system, and the AB follow-up cadence that turns a 10-email day into a 100-email day.
connor-murray-cold-email-assumptive-cade.author: DJ Wayne
connor-murray-cold-email-assumptive-cade.excerpt: Most cold email training falls into two failure camps: sales-enablement bloat (five paragraphs, MBA-in-psychology emails nobody reads) or LinkedIn-guru cuteness (fake personalization, fellow-eagle openers, manipulative warmth). Connor Murray's system rejects both. The email is three paragraphs — who I am, why I am relevant, what I want — written in assumptive language, sent from pre-built lists with persona-specific templates, and followed up every other day with three short notes that redirect attention back to the original. 70 to 80 percent of meetings come from the follow-ups, not the first email.

craig-elias-trigger-event-selling.author: DJ Wayne
craig-elias-trigger-event-selling.excerpt: Craig Elias literally wrote the book on trigger-event selling. His thesis: prospects do not buy because your email arrives — they buy because something in their world broke. There are three event types (Awareness, Bad experience, Change), buyers need three events stacked to actually transact, and the vendor who arrives first wins 74% of the deals. Cold outreach without trigger thinking is luck.

Pick the right model for the medium (CMYK / RGB / HSB), build palettes through structured frameworks, and proportion them with 60/30/10 — not by guessing what feels premium.

hormozi-newsletter-email-marketing-39-mi.author: DJ Wayne
hormozi-newsletter-email-marketing-39-mi.excerpt: This is *not* a cold email playbook. Hormozi is teaching how to email a list of people who opted in — the average ROI is 35–45x because the cost of acquisition was already paid. The frame is content-via-email: reward every action in the chain (open, read, click, reply), template the format so readers know what they're getting, and treat the email like a published artifact (plain text, mobile, no money-language, one CTA per audience).

kallaway-7-storytelling-mistakes.title: 7 Storytelling Mistakes: Lessons from Kane Kallaway
kallaway-7-storytelling-mistakes.description: A deep read of Kallaway's "7 Storytelling Mistakes That Are KILLING Small Creators" — the rolling peak/release arc that replaces the bell curve, the W-stack reorder, the rebuy mechanic, villain framing, and atomic shareability.
kallaway-7-storytelling-mistakes.author: DJ Wayne
kallaway-7-storytelling-mistakes.excerpt: Time compression has rewired audience attention. The traditional bell-curve story arc was built for captive audiences. On the internet, viewers re-decide every few seconds whether to keep watching. Modern storytelling is a rolling sequence of peaks and releases — and the seven mistakes here are the ways small creators still tell stories as if their viewer were trapped in a movie theater.
kallaway-7-storytelling-mistakes.sourceTitle: 7 Storytelling Mistakes That Are KILLING Small Creators

lineageSources.title: 7 Storytelling Mistakes That Are KILLING Small Creators

kallaway-hooks-impossible-to-skip.title: Hooks Impossible to Skip: Lessons from Kane Kallaway
kallaway-hooks-impossible-to-skip.seoTitle: Hooks Impossible to Skip: Kane Kallaway
kallaway-hooks-impossible-to-skip.description: A deep read of Kallaway's "Give me 15 mins, and I'll make your hooks impossible to skip" — the four-mistake diagnostic (Delay / Confusion / Irrelevance / Disinterest), the A vs B contrast formula, and a verbatim AI clarity-rewrite prompt.
kallaway-hooks-impossible-to-skip.author: DJ Wayne
kallaway-hooks-impossible-to-skip.excerpt: A hook has exactly one job: get the viewer to opt in and stay. Every failed hook fails because it commits one or more of four mistakes — Delay, Confusion, Irrelevance, Disinterest. Fixing hooks is not a creative-writing problem; it's a checklist of subtractive rewrites against those four failure modes.
kallaway-hooks-impossible-to-skip.sourceTitle: Give me 15 mins, and I'll make your hooks impossible to skip

kallaway-irresistible-hooks.title: Irresistible Hooks: Lessons from Kane Kallaway
kallaway-irresistible-hooks.description: A deep read of two Kane Kallaway hook videos — the three-beat build formula (context lean → scroll-stop interjection → contrarian snapback) and the four-mistake diagnostic (Delay / Confusion / Irrelevance / Disinterest), plus the A/B contrast formula and his AI clarity-rewrite prompt.
kallaway-irresistible-hooks.author: DJ Wayne
kallaway-irresistible-hooks.excerpt: A hook has one job: get the right viewer to opt in and stay. Kallaway gives you two tools for it — a three-beat formula to build a hook from scratch (Context Lean → Scroll-Stop Interjection → Contrarian Snapback) and a four-mistake checklist to fix one that isn't working (Delay, Confusion, Irrelevance, Disinterest). Build with the first; rewrite with the second.
kallaway-irresistible-hooks.sourceTitle: How to Create Irresistible Hooks (and blow up your content)

kallaway-master-storyteller.title: How to Become a Master Storyteller: Lessons from Kane Kallaway
kallaway-master-storyteller.seoTitle: How to Become a Master Storyteller: Kallaway
kallaway-master-storyteller.description: A deep read of Kallaway's "How To Become A Master Storyteller" — the six craft moves: dance (but/therefore), rhythm (Gary Provost), tone (one close friend), direction (write the ending first), lens (category-of-one), and hook (show while you tell).
kallaway-master-storyteller.author: DJ Wayne
kallaway-master-storyteller.excerpt: Storytelling is a learnable stack of six craft moves, not a personality trait. Stories perform when they alternate context and conflict, pace through varied sentence rhythm, hold attention via conversational tone, are written end-first, get differentiated through unique story lenses, and open with visual hooks that show before they tell.
kallaway-master-storyteller.sourceTitle: How To Become A Master Storyteller

kallaway-storytelling-genius-dopamine-la.title: The Dopamine Ladder: Kane Kallaway on Storytelling Genius
kallaway-storytelling-genius-dopamine-la.description: A deep read of Kallaway's "How to Become a Storytelling Genius (Dopamine Ladders)" — six rungs from pre-cognitive stimulation to Pavlovian revelation, the curiosity-anticipation-validation cycle, and why faceless content caps out at rung 4.
kallaway-storytelling-genius-dopamine-la.author: DJ Wayne
kallaway-storytelling-genius-dopamine-la.excerpt: A viewer's attention is not won in a single hook. It is won across six escalating dopamine releases — Stimulation, Captivation, Anticipation, Validation, Affection, Revelation — that move from raw visual stimulation up to a Pavlovian bond with the creator.
kallaway-storytelling-genius-dopamine-la.sourceTitle: How to Become a Storytelling Genius (Dopamine Ladders)

kole-jain-7-ui-ux-mistakes-beginner.title: 7 UI/UX Mistakes That Scream Beginner: Lessons from Kole Jain
kole-jain-7-ui-ux-mistakes-beginner.description: A deep read of Kole Jain's "7 UI/UX mistakes that SCREAM you're a beginner" — flow before pixels, the three-step shadow recipe, the 10px corner-radius standard, and why subtraction beats addition.
kole-jain-7-ui-ux-mistakes-beginner.author: DJ Wayne
kole-jain-7-ui-ux-mistakes-beginner.excerpt: Beginner UIs aren't broken from lack of skill — they're broken from a small set of repeatable, fixable mistakes. Polish comes from subtraction, consistency, and respect for the user's flow, not from adding more visual flair.
kole-jain-7-ui-ux-mistakes-beginner.sourceTitle: 7 UI/UX mistakes that SCREAM you're a beginner

lincoln-murphy-ideal-customer-profile-fr.title: Ideal Customer Profile: Lessons from Lincoln Murphy
lincoln-murphy-ideal-customer-profile-fr.description: A deep read of Lincoln Murphy's canonical ICP framework — the seven dimensions (Ready, Willing, Able, Success Potential, Acquisition Efficiency, Ascension, Advocacy), the six fit types inside Success Potential, why the ICP describes a situation rather than a permanent customer type, and how it differs from a buyer persona.
lincoln-murphy-ideal-customer-profile-fr.author: DJ Wayne
lincoln-murphy-ideal-customer-profile-fr.excerpt: Lincoln Murphy's ICP is the framework most other ICP frameworks descend from. It is not a buyer persona, not a list of titles, not a TAM number. It is a seven-dimension description of the customer who can succeed with your product right now — and the six fit types that decide whether they can succeed at all. Cold outreach without this is a list, not an ICP.

lineageSources.title: The Ideal Customer Profile (ICP) Framework
lineageSources.creator: Lincoln Murphy

mark-roberge-science-of-scaling-segment-.title: Science of Scaling: Lessons from Mark Roberge
mark-roberge-science-of-scaling-segment-.description: A deep read of Mark Roberge's revenue-growth framework — the aha-moment formula ("P% of customers achieve E events every T days"), the Green/Yellow/Red segment tiering, the Quality-x-Engagement lead grid, and why retention beats growth as the leading indicator of product-market fit.
mark-roberge-science-of-scaling-segment-.author: DJ Wayne
mark-roberge-science-of-scaling-segment-.excerpt: Mark Roberge's thesis is that most series-C failures are caused by scaling sales on top of an unfinished product-market-fit measurement. The fix is a single retention formula ("P% of customers achieve E events every T days") that becomes the leading indicator, a Green/Yellow/Red view of which segments actually have PMF, and a Quality × Engagement grid that rewards demo requests over ebook downloads. Cold outreach without a segment tier is wasted budget.

michael-seibel-cold-email-investors.title: Cold Emailing Investors: Lessons from Michael Seibel
michael-seibel-cold-email-investors.description: A deep read of Michael Seibel and Y Combinator on cold-emailing investors: short factual emails, no jargon, company-address credibility, optional deck discipline, tracking opens, no immediate meeting ask, and the right order of operations for starting an investor conversation.
michael-seibel-cold-email-investors.author: DJ Wayne
michael-seibel-cold-email-investors.excerpt: Michael Seibel reframes investor cold email around one goal: get the investor to understand enough to reply. The first email should not try to secure an investment, force a meeting, or tell a long origin story. It should take less than a minute to read, explain what the company does in simple language, show raw facts that matter to investors, and invite the back-and-forth.

lineageSources.title: How To Cold Email Investors
lineageSources.creator: Michael Seibel

nesrine-changuel-delightful-products-fra.title: Building Delightful Products: Nesrine Changuel's 4-Step Framework
nesrine-changuel-delightful-products-fra.description: A deep read of Nesrine Changuel on Lenny's Podcast — the 4-step Delight Model, the three pillars (remove friction, anticipate needs, exceed expectations), the 50/40/10 roadmap rule, and why anti-delight destroys trust faster than no delight.
nesrine-changuel-delightful-products-fra.author: DJ Wayne
nesrine-changuel-delightful-products-fra.excerpt: Delight is a strategy, not confetti. A product that solves a functional need AND honors an emotional need at the same time — that's deep delight, and it's the differentiator in any crowded category. The framework: motivators → opportunities → delight grid → checklist.
nesrine-changuel-delightful-products-fra.sourceTitle: A 4-step framework for building delightful products

lineageSources.title: A 4-step framework for building delightful products

sam-mckenna-show-me-you-know-me-ai-era.title: AI-Era Show Me You Know Me: Lessons from Sam McKenna
sam-mckenna-show-me-you-know-me-ai-era.seoTitle: Show Me You Know Me in the AI Era: Sam McKenna
sam-mckenna-show-me-you-know-me-ai-era.description: A deep read of Sam McKenna on why seller-centered cold calling and spray-and-pray email underperform modern buyer behavior, and how Show Me You Know Me research, LinkedIn signals, authentic personal connection, thread-based nurturing, and buyer-timed follow-up create executive replies.
sam-mckenna-show-me-you-know-me-ai-era.author: DJ Wayne
sam-mckenna-show-me-you-know-me-ai-era.excerpt: Sam McKenna frames the modern outbound problem as a buyer-journey problem, not a channel problem. Cold calls interrupt. Spray-and-pray email signals seller self-interest. Show Me You Know Me earns the right to the meeting by proving the seller invested real attention before asking for time. The strongest ideas here are going higher in the org, using LinkedIn and public interviews as research surfaces, making personal hooks feel authentic instead of pasted, and nurturing replies in the original thread when executives are actually working.
sam-mckenna-show-me-you-know-me-ai-era.sourceTitle: 100th Episode: The End of the Line for Cold Calling (& What's Replacing it) -- with Sam McKenna

lineageSources.title: 100th Episode: The End of the Line for Cold Calling (& What's Replacing it) -- with Sam McKenna
lineageSources.creator: Sam McKenna

steli-efti-low-friction-replies-123.title: Low-Friction Replies: Lessons from Steli Efti
steli-efti-low-friction-replies-123.description: A deep read of Steli Efti and Close on the 1-2-3 email hack: why silence is the enemy, how reply-with-a-number options reduce buyer friction, where the tactic fits in top-of-funnel qualification and ghosted follow-up, and how to use it without becoming gimmicky.
steli-efti-low-friction-replies-123.author: DJ Wayne
steli-efti-low-friction-replies-123.excerpt: Steli Efti starts from a blunt operating truth: the worst cold-email outcome is silence. The 1-2-3 hack works by turning a vague reply burden into a finite choice. Instead of asking the buyer to compose a thoughtful answer, the email names three or four plausible situations and asks them to reply with a number. Used carefully, this becomes a qualification tool, a dead-lead revival tool, and an objection-discovery tool.
steli-efti-low-friction-replies-123.sourceTitle: Get 457% more replies to your sales emails with the 1, 2, 3 hack

tuan-le-3-billion-views-psychology.title: What Getting 3 Billion Views Taught Me About Human Psychology: Lessons from Tuan Le
tuan-le-3-billion-views-psychology.description: A deep read of Tuan Le's brand-content psychology framework: format recognition, curiosity gaps, identity layers, credential shortcuts, share-as-status, and the Hook -> Problem -> Story -> Payoff skeleton.
tuan-le-3-billion-views-psychology.author: DJ Wayne
tuan-le-3-billion-views-psychology.excerpt: Virality is not about the product being exciting. Tuan Le's framework argues that the viewer's brain runs six fast filters before conscious evaluation: familiar format, curiosity gap, identity relevance, credential, sharer payoff, and story.
tuan-le-3-billion-views-psychology.sourceTitle: What Getting 3 Billion Views Taught Me About Human Psychology

underscore-vc-minimum-viable-segment.title: Minimum Viable Segment: Lessons from Underscore VC (Michael Skok)
underscore-vc-minimum-viable-segment.seoTitle: Minimum Viable Segment: Michael Skok's Framework
underscore-vc-minimum-viable-segment.description: A deep read of the Minimum Viable Segment (MVS) framework from Underscore VC and Michael Skok — the three criteria a segment must meet (Common Needs, Dominability, Viability with MVP), the disqualifiers that pull a segment out of scope, and why divergent needs are the real reason MVPs fail to land.
underscore-vc-minimum-viable-segment.author: DJ Wayne
underscore-vc-minimum-viable-segment.excerpt: Most early-stage outreach fails not because the email was bad but because the segment was incoherent. The Minimum Viable Segment framework says a segment is valid only if it passes three tests: customers share a common need, the segment is small enough to dominate, and your current product can serve it without divergent customization. Cold outreach run against a segment that fails any of these is wasted.

buildos-for-adhd-minds.title: BuildOS for Scattered Minds: When Your Working Memory Needs a Backup
buildos-for-adhd-minds.seoTitle: BuildOS for ADHD and Scattered Minds
buildos-for-adhd-minds.description: A working guide for people whose ideas move faster than their tools. How to externalize working memory into a thinking environment that remembers, so your brain does not have to.
buildos-for-adhd-minds.author: DJ Wayne
buildos-for-adhd-minds.excerpt: Your brain does not lose ideas. Your tools do. For people whose thoughts move faster than their hands, here is how to externalize working memory into a system that remembers — so you can capture the idea now and find it later.

buildos-for-developers.title: BuildOS for Developers: A System That Survives the Context Switch
buildos-for-developers.description: A working guide for engineers and indie builders. How to keep side projects, decisions, and the why behind the code in one thinking environment that your AI tools can actually use.
buildos-for-developers.author: DJ Wayne
buildos-for-developers.excerpt: You think in systems but live in chaos. Twelve repos, three AI chats with conflicting plans, and the reason you made that decision lives nowhere. Here is how developers use BuildOS to keep the why connected to the work.

buildos-for-technical-project-managers.title: BuildOS for Technical Project Managers: Hold the Whole Portfolio Without Drowning in It
buildos-for-technical-project-managers.description: A working guide for TPMs and program managers. How to keep goals, plans, risks, and the why behind every call in one thinking environment that remembers across projects.
buildos-for-technical-project-managers.author: DJ Wayne
buildos-for-technical-project-managers.excerpt: You are the human integration layer between five workstreams, three stakeholders, and a roadmap that changes weekly. Here is how technical project managers use BuildOS to hold the whole picture without becoming the bottleneck.

writing-in-the-ai-age-how-writers-can-be.title: Writing in the AI Age: How Writers Can Be Prolific
writing-in-the-ai-age-how-writers-can-be.seoTitle: Writing in the AI Age: How to Be Prolific
writing-in-the-ai-age-how-writers-can-be.description: The prolific writers are not generating more. They are losing less. A method for staying prolific in the AI age — built from how the most productive writers actually work.
writing-in-the-ai-age-how-writers-can-be.author: DJ Wayne
writing-in-the-ai-age-how-writers-can-be.excerpt: You are not unproductive because you lack discipline or the right app. You are unproductive because you keep losing your own thinking. Here is how the most prolific writers stay prolific — and how to do it in the AI age.
